proposal_text:
Proposal number: 1333885

Proposal: LET'S WALK! - climate change awareness day event

Summary


Purpose: helping to rise the climate change awareness our world so urgently needs. 

Suggested actions: During that day we encourage people to walk and to:

leave their cars at home,
skip the bus,
avoid the subway and
turn to their own bodies in order to move around the city, by walking, by using their bicycles, their rollers, their skates.
If you are not lucky enough to be living near your work space you can still collaborate by doing home office and other activities such as walking to the grocery or the club.
Using a smartphone app we intend to track how much people walk during the day of the event. This information will then be published by social media and traditional media. The data provided by the app will also be translate in the reductions already mentioned, calculating savings in relationship  with the kilometrers walked and avoiding using transportation during that day.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page [14].
Because of the altruistic spirit of the cause, we expect to have support from the local ONG´s, health organizations and the government.     
Main value generation loops v1.0.png


Short term Impacts: we expect to save energy and money, GHG emissions, and we will contribute to build sustainable habits, for a healthy environment in the war against the dangers of a sedentary lifestyle.  

Long term impacts:

Since the proposal is to make people aware of how important it is to care about the planet. More climate change aware people would naturally support the following initiatives:

Recycling: We forecast that it could increase by 10 %,
 
Energy savings: recommendations use increment by 10 %
 
Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law: is approved and covers 5 % of city demand.
 
Electric buses: their use is implemented in 2 % of city bus lines.
Is this proposal for a practice or a project?
Practice

What actions do you propose?
a) The main thrust of “Let’s Walk” is walking, because Buenos Aires has a high population density, and distances might be shorter than in other cities, so walking to work is a real possibility for some people. People can

Walk 10 - 15 blocks to their offices or back home.

Use public transportation, including buses, underground / subways and then walk the rest of the way to their offices or homes.

 

b) Cycling also adds value to the “Let’s walk” proposal for the population 10 to 30 blocks from their office. The Buenos Aires City already has implemented an “ecobici” (eco bikes) program [15]. The ecobici system has bikes parked in several distributed city points, and anyone can use one of these bikes for free using his / her document. You just return the bike in another bike parking place near your destination.

 

REVIEW OF SOME “BIKE TO WORK” PROGRAMS IN CANADA AND USA:

USA Today informs that Bike to Work programs might be rising in US [21].

Through a google search we found the following relevant links:

table-bike-to-work-projects.png


ANSWERS TO SOME OBJECTIONS TO “BIKE TO WORK”

The Huffpost article [16] mentions 9 objections to “Bike to work”, here are our thoughts about them!

main-objections.png

“LET’S WALK BUSINESS MODEL CANVAS”

LET`S WALK PROJECT BUSINESS CANVAS v2.0.png

As we know we have different partners in this event, which play a fundamental role.

On one side, the people enrolled in the event, that will participate registering using a mobile app. They will either walk to their works or commute by bike. In order to be eligible to receive a prize, each participant will allow the app to register information about distances walked / biked, which transportation they usually use to commute in order to calculate GHG emission savings due to the event. Those people that contribute with the biggest GHG emission savings will win prizes as well as  benefits from companies that sponsor the event.

It´s necessary to distinguish, that by incentivizing the movement we are preventing obesity, decrease bad cholesterol and increase good cholesterol, improving the state of mind and incorporating healthy habits, so typically HMOs and the city government will be very much interested, as in Argentina poor people use public hospitals maintained by city taxes.

On the other side, we need to be able to organize this event with which we need the Government's support to convey the event to the society. It will be benefited by an increased use of the public transport by the population, better flow of the traffic (less cars in the streets)  and at the same time it will reaffirm it´s commitment with the environment.

In addition to this, it will be a great opportunity to advertise the use of the eco-bikes(“ecobicis”) [15] and, as a result, the bike lanes.

On the other hand, the information generated by the app (big data)  will be of great utility since it will show which are the population's needs (for example: the best locations for entrances of subways , routes of buses or even which are the areas that are disconnected and need public transport).

It is also important to have the companies that will join on this great event allowing the use of “home office”. In addition, they will collaborate as sponsors and obtain in exchange Big Data to improve its business and find niche.

Sponsorships are one of the best ways of promoting a business and winning popularity between the target and generate good marketing for a brand. It can be specially used to increase credibility, image and prestige of the brand.

All participation is voluntary, and public transportation will still be available to those who want to use it.

LGC Team will ask for suggestions and advice from NGOs(such as WWF, who implemented "The earth hour"). In order to have more tools to guide the team on how to develop the project. 
 
Once LCG Team recieves feedback from NGOs.They will present the formal proposal to the Institute of Technology of Buenos Aires (ITBA) and to the MIT Colab
 
The team is looking forward to receive feedback from CLIMATE COLAB in order to improve the project. ITBA will give their consent and support to it.
 
We received the recognition and prize from MIT Colab thanks to the backing through votes from ITBA.
 
LGC Team will contact several organizations to help with fundraising and to add value to the final event (GOOGLE, MASS MEDIA, SPONSORS, etc). Nevertheless to make it happen we need firstly the GOVERNMENT authorization.
 
The government will notificate the companies of the proposal and ask for their cooperation by allowing the employees to work from home. They will also have to arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes.
 
Distance walked and GHG savings: To have an estimation of the GHGs saved is necessary an app (develop by GOOGLE if possible) that will measure them.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page.But it is important to keep record of the route like Google Maps does. 
 
Once the proposal is accepted the next step is to settle a date and communicate it to the society, a key actor in this process is MASS MEDIA. 
 
The day of the event SPONSORS will play an important role by motivating the population to walk more. This could be achived by offering refreshments and prizes. 
 
Finally,all the information is collected by GOOGLE. Including steps per person and the equivalence with the reduction of GHGs obteined.
 
The LGC Team will analyze the results and make papers to be published and to communicate it to the community, the government and the MIT. 
 
As the results will be positive this idea can be implemented in other countries, all over the world. By this the proposal will reach a greater impact and a change in people's habits worldwide.


Who will take these actions?
LGC Team at ITBA: designs proposal and presents it to Climate Colab.
 
Argentine Government sponsors "LET'S WALK day"
 
Media covers the events publishing metrics on energy saved, emissions saved and money saved.
 
5 % local city population participates in the "LET'S WALK day"
 
NGOs sponsor event.
 
Transportation works as usual.

Other countries can implement the proposal to have a higher impact
Where will these actions be taken?
City level: The first objective is to do it in Buenos Aires City as is the most populated region, the most contaminating one and the principal concentration of companies.

Country level: Later by the positive results our goal is to carry out the experience at country level, in cities like Cordoba, Rosario, Mendoza and San Luis.

Worldwide: Finally, other countries can use the proposal to make their own impacts since everything counts.

 

In addition, specify the country or countries where these actions will be taken.
Argentina

Country 2
Brazil

Country 3
Chile

Country 4
United States

Country 5
No country selected

Impact/Benefits
What impact will these actions have on greenhouse gas emissions and/or adapting to climate change?
1) City of Buenos Aires

a) Short term CO2e emissions saved in Buenos Aires city:

We postulate that 5 % of the population will participate in the event.
Public transportation use: 80% of population [1]

 
Emissions saved by cars commuting from Buenos Aires Province:

10410352 cars/month [2] /30 days/month x 15 km/trip  x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] x 0.05 ( 5 % population participates  in event) = 26 tCO2e (metric tons) / day.
 

Emission saved by cars inside the city:

2.89 million people x 5% people participation x 20 % people use cars x 7 km average trip to work x 2 trips /day x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] = 40.46 tCO2e / day
 

Total short term CO2e savings = (26 + 40.46) = 66.46 tCO2e /day

b) Long term CHG savings (5 years) in Buenos Aires city:

We forecast that recycling could increase by 10 %. Today only 23% of Buenos Aires city recycle [4]. And recycling saves from 0.34 to 10 Tonnes of CO2e from each ton of recycled material [5]. Considering an average 1 Tonne of CO2e saved per Tonne of recycled material, current Buenos Aires savings from recycling would be 976 Tonnes of domiciliary residues [6] x 23% people recycle x 0.1 (10 % recycling increase ) x 1 tCO2e/1 t recycled material = 22 Tonnes of CO2e / year saved.
 
Energy savings recommendations [7] use increment by 10 %. The average electricity consumption per electrified household in Argentina is 3600 kWh [11], so we estimate that the savings will be of 360 kWh/home per year * 963 thousand homes * 0.05 (5 % of participation) = 17334 kWh per year. This could save up to 17334 kWh x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 10.2 tCO2e/year
Distributed solar photo voltaic electricity law is approved and covers 1 % of city demand.
2.89 million people x 4100 kWh/person [10] x 0.01 (1% of demand) x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 70205 tCO2e/year
 
It incentivates the use of public transport in this way, so the Government must make it more efficient.For example, the use of electric buses is implemented in 2 % of city.[8] [9] Each bus will save 175 metrics tones of pollutant emissions and 22.700 liters of fuel per unit and year. We estimate that there are 10200 buses in Buenos Aires,so 2% is 204, the total reduction will be 204*175 mtones= 35700 metric tones of pollutant / year saved.[10]
 
Total emission savings = (22 + 10.2 + 70205 + 35700) = 105937 tCO2e/year

2) Extrapolation to world:

a) Short term:

66.46 tCO2e/(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 24997 tCO2e

b) Long term:

105.937 tCO2e/year /(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 39 million tCO2e / year.

The transportation sector accounts from 19 % to 25 % of total GHGs emissions, according to [12] and [13]

So the total GHG due to the transportation sector may be calculated as

46.000 million tCO2e x 22% = 10.120 million tCO2e

So the proposal savings would be 39 / 10120 = 0.38 % of the transportation sector emissions.

What are other key benefits?
Generate awareness on using more public transport
Government will have to improve the efficiency of public transport
Change the habits of the society towards a sustainable and ecological life
Smog and sound pollution will be reduced
People´s stress levels will be reduced
Increase the use of bicycles
Change habits and weaken sedentary lifestyle
Cut costs of transport and so combustibles
By implemeting home-office besides helping this cause, the employeers will have a cut down of their costs
Decrease GHGs emissions by reducing the use of cars and public transport
Costs/Challenges
What are the proposal’s projected costs?
1) Cost of security forces involved in organizing the event.

2) Cost of health support personnel, ambulances, paramedics,

3) Cost of traditional media ads

4) Cost of blog, SN ads and  social media managers / influencers

5) Cost of winner prizes

6) Cost of collaboration agreement with smartphone apps that calculate distances walked by people participating in the event

7) Cost of webpage that summarizes energy savings, money saved, GHGs emission saved.

Timeline
The day of the event, there will be an immediate impact by the reduction of the GHGs emissions.
In the short-term we are looking forward to make people realize that their can make a huge change on a daily basis.For example, they can avoid the use of cars in short distances.
Over more, we can affirm that one of important change will be the use bikes. Nowadays, Buenos Aires city provides free bikes and bicicles in the downtown. Our idea is to massify these kind of habits and became a green country free of sedentarism.
What's more, we want to Incentive companies to stablish a home-office day. This will allow people who live far from their jobs to colaborate to this cause.
Medium-term: These "Homeoffice days" could at first be once a year, but once people catch the habit, it could be done monthly.
Long-term: Eventually people could do these "Homeoffice days" weekly, so that people enjoy life a little more and savings of energy, money and GHGs emissions are multiplied.

feasibility.rationale: The event requires coordination with multiple stakeholders (government, NGOs, media, app developer) and assumes 5 % of Buenos Aires residents (≈145 k people) will voluntarily participate. While such mobilization is plausible with strong incentives, the proposal lacks detailed risk mitigation, budget breakdown, and concrete implementation timelines, creating moderate uncertainty about execution.

novelty.rationale: One‑day car‑free or walk‑to‑work events and gamified tracking apps already exist (e.g., World Car Free Day, Walk to Work campaigns). The combination of home‑office encouragement and a prize‑based app is incremental rather than fundamentally new.

impact.rationale: Short‑term CO₂e savings of ~66 t day⁻¹ are modest relative to city‑wide emissions. Long‑term impact estimates (e.g., 10 % increase in recycling, 1 % solar law adoption) rely on unverified behavioral spill‑over from a single event, making the causal chain speculative and the extrapolation to 39 Mt yr⁻¹ globally highly uncertain.

presentation.rationale: The proposal conveys the core idea and includes quantitative back‑of‑the‑envelope calculations, but suffers from inconsistent formatting, grammatical errors, and insufficient citation detail, which hampers clear assessment.

A1.perspective_summary: The proposal outlines a city‑wide ‘Let’s Walk’ day that encourages walking, cycling, and home‑office participation, tracked via a smartphone app to quantify GHG savings. While the concept is socially appealing, the evidence base for lasting behavioral change and the quantitative impact claims are weak, and implementation details remain vague.
key_concerns: Assumed 5 % participation may be optimistic for a first‑time event.
Long‑term behavior change (recycling, solar adoption, electric buses) is not empirically linked to a single day’s activity.
GHG savings calculations depend on generic emission factors and do not account for rebound effects (e.g., increased car use on other days).
Data privacy and accuracy of the tracking app are not addressed.
Budget, staffing, and risk mitigation plans are absent, limiting feasibility assessment.

feasibility.rationale: The event requires multiple inter‑agency approvals (city government, transport authority, public safety, data‑privacy regulator) and a partnership with a private app developer. While Buenos Aires already runs bike‑share programs, the need for a new app, prize incentives, and large‑scale media outreach creates logistical and budgetary hurdles that make implementation plausible but not assured.

novelty.rationale: Car‑free or walk‑to‑work days have been organized in many cities; using a smartphone app for individual GHG accounting adds a modest twist, but the core concept is not new.

impact.rationale: The proposal quantifies a short‑term reduction of ~66 tCO₂e/day and a long‑term annual saving of ~106 ktCO₂e, representing <0.5 % of the transport sector’s emissions. The numbers are plausible for a one‑day pilot, yet the extrapolation to national or global impact rests on uncertain behavior change and scaling assumptions.

presentation.rationale: The document contains extensive calculations and references but suffers from poor organization, typographical errors, and unclear sections (e.g., mixed timelines, duplicated stakeholder lists). This hampers quick policy assessment.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance standpoint, LET'S WALK is a feasible pilot if the city secures inter‑agency coordination, addresses data‑privacy rules, and allocates modest funding for safety and communications. The idea is only modestly novel, and its projected climate impact, while measurable, remains limited and highly dependent on sustained behavioral change. The proposal’s presentation needs refinement to support clear decision‑making.
key_concerns: Data‑privacy compliance for location tracking and personal emissions data (national data‑protection law, potential need for consent management).
Securing formal authorisation from the municipal government and transport authority to modify routes or encourage home‑office on the event day.
Funding and procurement of the mobile app, especially if a major tech firm (e.g., Google) is involved, which may raise procurement and conflict‑of‑interest issues.
Ensuring public safety (traffic management, medical support) for a large number of pedestrians and cyclists on a single day.
Equity considerations for residents who cannot walk or bike due to disability, distance, or safety concerns; the policy must include accommodations.
Reliability of the emissions‑saving algorithm and transparency of the methodology to avoid overstating impact.

feasibility.rationale: The event builds on existing car‑free/walk‑to‑work concepts, so the core idea is doable. However, it hinges on a custom smartphone app, large‑scale coordination with government, NGOs, sponsors, and security/health services, and assumes 5 % city participation without a clear funding or rollout plan. These gaps make large‑scale execution moderately risky.

novelty.rationale: Walking‑day campaigns and app‑based gamified tracking are already common (e.g., World Car Free Day, bike‑to‑work challenges). The proposal does not introduce a fundamentally new technology or behavioral mechanism.

impact.rationale: The quantitative estimates (≈66 tCO₂e saved in one day, 105 k tCO₂e/yr long‑term) rely on optimistic participation and behavior‑change assumptions. If achieved, the awareness and habit shift could be valuable, but the actual emissions reduction is likely modest relative to the city’s total transport footprint.

presentation.rationale: The document is lengthy, contains typographical errors, inconsistent tables, and calculations that are not fully explained, making it difficult for reviewers to verify claims quickly.

A3.perspective_summary: From an operational standpoint, LET'S WALK! is a feasible one‑day awareness event that leverages existing walking and bike‑share infrastructure, but it requires substantial coordination, clear funding, and a reliable app platform. The concept is not novel, and while the projected emissions savings could be meaningful if participation targets are met, the assumptions are optimistic. The proposal’s presentation hampers quick assessment.
key_concerns: Unclear funding source for app development, incentives, security, and health personnel.
Data‑privacy and accuracy of the tracking app are not addressed.
Government authorization and logistical support (e.g., road closures, crowd management) are assumed but not detailed.
Participation rate of 5 % of Buenos Aires residents may be unrealistic without a strong outreach plan.
Cost estimates are vague; a detailed budget and risk mitigation plan are missing.

feasibility.rationale: The event can be organized with existing city resources (eco‑bikes, media, NGOs) and a simple smartphone app, but it depends on multiple external approvals (government authorization, sponsor funding, security forces) and assumes that 5 % of the city can realistically walk or bike to work in one day, which may be optimistic for residents who live far from their jobs or lack safe routes.

novelty.rationale: Car‑free or walk‑to‑work days have been run elsewhere; the added element of a gamified app and prize incentives is a modest twist but not a fundamentally new concept.

impact.rationale: Projected emission savings (≈66 tCO₂e/day, 0.38 % of transport sector) are modest and hinge on the 5 % participation assumption. Health benefits and awareness gains could be meaningful for participants, yet many low‑income residents may be excluded because they cannot safely walk or lack bikes, limiting equitable impact.

presentation.rationale: The proposal contains many typographical errors, duplicated sections, and unclear tables, making it hard for community members to grasp the core actions and benefits without additional clarification.

A4.perspective_summary: From the viewpoint of Buenos Aires residents, the "Let's Walk" day is a well‑intentioned awareness event that could boost health and modestly cut emissions if enough people can safely replace car trips with walking or biking. However, the plan overlooks accessibility barriers for those living far from work, safety of pedestrian infrastructure, and privacy concerns around the tracking app, which may limit broad community acceptance.
key_concerns: Equity: many low‑income workers rely on cars or long public‑transport trips and cannot realistically walk or bike the required distance.
Safety: insufficient sidewalks or protected bike lanes could deter participation and raise accident risk.
Privacy: the app will collect detailed location and travel data; participants may be reluctant to share this information.
Dependence on government and sponsor approvals, which could delay or cancel the event.
Sustainability of incentives: prizes may attract one‑off participation but not lasting behavior change.

feasibility.rationale: The event relies on multiple actors (government, NGOs, sponsors, app developer) and a one‑day coordination effort, which is plausible but contingent on securing permissions, funding, and a functional tracking app; these dependencies lower certainty.

novelty.rationale: Awareness walks and car‑free days are common; the addition of a prize‑based app is incremental rather than a fundamentally new approach to climate action.

impact.rationale: Estimated emissions savings amount to ~0.38 % of transport sector emissions and depend on sustained behavior change that is not demonstrated; short‑term gains are modest and long‑term effects are speculative.

presentation.rationale: The proposal contains many typographical errors, duplicated sections, and unclear tables, making it difficult to extract key assumptions and metrics.

A5.perspective_summary: From an equity and justice standpoint, the "Let's Walk" event primarily benefits urban residents who can afford smartphones, live near work, and have the option to work from home, while the costs of security, media, and data infrastructure fall on public funds and sponsors. The design risks excluding low‑income commuters, people with disabilities, or those in peripheral neighborhoods who lack safe walking routes or flexible jobs, and it raises privacy concerns about the collection of location data by a corporate partner.
key_concerns: Distributional equity: participation assumes proximity to work, access to smartphones, and ability to take a home‑office day, which many low‑income or peripheral workers lack.
Data privacy: the app will collect detailed movement data; governance, consent, and data use by Google need explicit safeguards.
Infrastructure safety: without guaranteed safe sidewalks or bike lanes, encouraging walking could expose vulnerable pedestrians to traffic hazards.
Sponsor influence: reliance on corporate sponsors for prizes and data analytics may bias the agenda and limit public oversight.
Assumption of lasting behavior change: the proposal does not provide evidence that a single event will translate into durable reductions in car use.

feasibility.rationale: The event relies on coordinated government authorization, sponsor funding, and a custom smartphone app to track walking/biking. While a one‑day walk campaign is logistically possible, the proposal assumes 5 % population participation and seamless app development without detailed risk mitigation, making feasibility moderate.

novelty.rationale: Encouraging walking and biking on a dedicated day is not new, but coupling it with a real‑time emissions‑tracking app and prize incentives adds a modestly novel data‑driven element.

impact.rationale: Calculated short‑term savings of ~66 tCO₂e/day and long‑term savings of ~106 ktCO₂e/year for Buenos Aires represent a small fraction of total transport emissions (≈0.38 %). The potential for habit formation could amplify impact, but the proposal’s quantitative claims rely on optimistic participation and behavior‑change assumptions.

presentation.rationale: The document contains extensive calculations and visual references, but suffers from poor organization, typographical errors, and unclear linkage between sections, which hampers clear communication of the plan.

A6.perspective_summary: From a long‑term sustainability viewpoint, the "Let's Walk!" event is a feasible, moderately novel awareness campaign that could generate modest emissions reductions and foster healthier, lower‑carbon commuting habits if participation targets are met. However, its systemic impact hinges on sustained behavior change and reliable data collection, which are not fully substantiated.
key_concerns: Assumed 5 % population participation may be overly optimistic without detailed outreach strategy.
Reliance on a custom app for accurate GHG savings calculations lacks validation and may introduce measurement error.
Potential rebound effects (e.g., increased car use after the event) are not addressed.
Equity considerations: low‑income residents may lack access to bikes or safe walking routes.
Long‑term habit persistence is assumed but not supported by a concrete behavior‑change framework.

peer_questions.question: Can you clarify the source and justification for the 0.1 kg CO₂eq per km emission factor used in the proposal's short‑term GHG savings calculation, given that typical passenger‑car emission factors in Argentina are often reported as higher?

You highlight that the 5 % city‑wide participation assumption may be optimistic; are there empirical data from prior one‑day car‑free or walking events in Buenos Aires or similar Latin American contexts that support achieving this participation level without substantial incentives?

Could you specify which Argentine data‑protection regulations (e.g., Law 25.326) would apply to the location‑tracking app, and what concrete compliance measures (consent, anonymisation, data‑retention limits) the proposal would need to implement?

In your view, which municipal departments or existing inter‑agency frameworks in Buenos Aires could realistically coordinate the event’s logistics (road closures, security, health services), and what formal agreements or mandates would be required to secure their participation?

Given your concern about the lack of a detailed risk‑mitigation plan, what specific mitigation actions would you prioritize (e.g., contingency staffing, insurance, communication protocols) to reduce implementation risk for the event?

You highlight data‑privacy and procurement challenges for the tracking app; do you have a realistic cost estimate for developing or licensing such an app and how could the city budget for it while ensuring compliance?

Given the privacy concerns you highlighted, what concrete data‑governance safeguards (e.g., consent mechanisms, data minimisation, independent oversight) could be built into the tracking app to protect participants, especially those from vulnerable communities?

You note the need for a behavior‑change framework to sustain habits; could you suggest specific, low‑cost outreach or incentive strategies that would engage low‑income commuters and people in peripheral neighborhoods who may lack safe walking routes or bike access?
What concrete metrics or methods would you use to evaluate whether the event disproportionately excludes low‑income residents who lack safe walking or biking infrastructure?
Do you have suggestions for a post‑event monitoring framework that could detect rebound effects (e.g., increased car use) and ensure the claimed long‑term habit changes actually materialize?

You highlighted equity and safety concerns; could you elaborate on mechanisms the proposal could adopt to monitor and mitigate possible rebound effects, where participants might increase car travel on non‑event days after the walk day?

Given the privacy issues you raised, what concrete data‑governance model (e.g., open‑source, independent audit, data‑trust) would you recommend to ensure the location data collected by the app remains transparent, secure, and under public oversight?

peer_answers.question: Can you clarify the source and justification for the 0.1 kg CO₂eq per km emission factor used in the proposal's short‑term GHG savings calculation, given that typical passenger‑car emission factors in Argentina are often reported as higher?
peer_answers.answer: The 0.1 kg CO₂eq / km factor appears to be a generic average often quoted for a mixed fleet of passenger cars in temperate regions, but Argentine vehicle inventories tend to be older and less efficient, leading to higher per‑kilometre emissions (often 0.13–0.18 kg CO₂eq / km according to the Argentine Ministry of Environment’s 2022 transport inventory). For a robust policy assessment the proposal should reference a specific national source—e.g., the "Inventario Nacional de Emisiones de Gases de Efecto Invernadero 2022"—or, if that data is unavailable, use a range (0.12–0.16 kg CO₂eq / km) and conduct a sensitivity analysis. Using a lower factor underestimates the potential savings and may affect the cost‑benefit narrative, so the proposal should either adopt the higher, locally‑validated factor or clearly justify why a lower, perhaps "well‑to‑do" factor is appropriate (e.g., if the calculation only counts average passenger‑car occupancy of 1.5 persons, the per‑person factor would be lower).

peer_answers.question: You highlight data‑privacy and procurement challenges for the tracking app; do you have a realistic cost estimate for developing or licensing such an app and how could the city budget for it while ensuring compliance?
peer_answers.answer:
A baseline cost estimate for a city‑scale activity‑tracking app with geolocation, emissions‑calculation engine, and prize‑management features can be derived from comparable public‑sector projects:

1. **Core development (in‑house or contracted)** – 150 k–250 k USD for a minimum‑viable product (MVP) covering Android/iOS, API integration with a GIS service (e.g., OpenStreetMap), and a simple dashboard. 
2. **Data‑privacy compliance** – 30 k–50 k USD for legal review, consent‑management module, and alignment with Argentina’s Personal Data Protection Law (PDPL) and any municipal data‑governance framework. 
3. **Hosting & maintenance (12 months)** – 20 k–35 k USD (cloud services, security monitoring, bug fixes). 
4. **User‑engagement layer (prizes, gamification)** – 25 k–40 k USD (design, integration with sponsor APIs). 

**Total rough budget:** **≈ USD 250 k–375 k** for a one‑year pilot.

**Funding pathways and compliance mechanisms:**
- **Municipal innovation fund** – many Argentine cities have dedicated budgets for smart‑city pilots; the proposal could be framed as a "digital public‑service" to tap that line.
- **Co‑funding with sponsors** – corporate sponsors (e.g., Google, local banks) can cover the prize pool and a portion of development in exchange for branding, provided the procurement follows the "public‑private partnership" (PPP) guidelines in the Argentine Public Procurement Law (Law 13.064). A transparent RFP process (open, limited‑tender) would mitigate conflict‑of‑interest concerns.
- **Grant programmes** – climate‑action funds from the Ministry of Environment or international bodies (e.g., GCF, UNDP) often earmark resources for data‑driven awareness campaigns; the app budget could be bundled into a larger grant application.
- **Compliance checklist** – before any contract, the city should:
  * Conduct a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA).
  * Ensure the app stores only anonymised, aggregated location data; raw trajectories must be deleted after the event or retained only with explicit consent.
  * Include clauses for third‑party audit and open‑source code release where feasible, to increase transparency and public trust.

By embedding these cost items into the municipal budget line for "Sustainable Mobility & Digital Innovation" and following the standard procurement steps (public notice, evaluation criteria, award), the city can realistically finance the app while remaining compliant with both procurement and data‑privacy regulations.

**Score impact:** The clarification of app cost does not materially alter the earlier feasibility rating (3). The need for a dedicated budget and procurement process was already reflected in the feasibility assessment; the more precise cost range simply confirms that the financial hurdle is moderate but manageable with existing municipal financing tools.

peer_answers.question: You highlight that the 5 % city‑wide participation assumption may be optimistic; are there empirical data from prior one‑day car‑free or walking events in Buenos Aires or similar Latin American contexts that support achieving this participation level without substantial incentives?
peer_answers.answer:
The limited published data from Buenos Aires suggest that one‑day car‑free initiatives have historically attracted far fewer participants than 5 % of the metropolitan population. For example, the "Dia sin Auto" pilot run in 2018 (organized by the Secretaría de Transporte) reported roughly 30 000 volunteers, which corresponds to about 0.3 % of the city’s 9 million residents. A similar "Ciclovía" event in Bogotá, Colombia, attracted around 200 000 cyclists and walkers in a city of 7.5 million (≈2.7 %). In Mexico City, the annual "Dia del Peatón" typically sees participation rates in the 1‑2 % range, based on counts from the Secretaría de Movilidad. These figures indicate that without a strong incentive structure (prizes, media blitz, employer‑driven participation) and extensive outreach, achieving a 5 % turnout is unlikely. To reach that level, the proposal would need to incorporate proven levers such as:
1. **Employer commitments** – a formal pledge from a coalition of large firms to allow a "walk‑to‑work" day, which in other Latin American pilots boosted participation by 2‑3 pp.
2. **Mass media and influencer campaigns** – coordinated TV, radio, and social‑media pushes that have been shown to double turnout in similar events.
3. **Tangible rewards** – prize draws, public recognition, or discounts on public‑transport passes, which are common in bike‑to‑work challenges and raise participation by 1‑2 pp.
In short, the 5 % assumption is not supported by existing evidence unless the proposal adds a robust incentive and outreach package.

Given the current lack of such a package, my feasibility score remains at **3** (moderately feasible) and the impact score stays at **3**.

peer_answers.question: In your view, which municipal departments or existing inter‑agency frameworks in Buenos Aires could realistically coordinate the event’s logistics (road closures, security, health services), and what formal agreements or mandates would be required to secure their participation?
peer_answers.answer:
Buenos Aires already operates a multi‑agency "Movilidad Sustentable" framework that can be leveraged for a city‑wide "Let’s Walk" day. The key entities and the type of formal arrangements needed are:

1. **Secretaría de Transporte (Transport Secretariat)** – responsible for public‑transport scheduling, bike‑lane management, and temporary road closures. A *Convenio de Cooperación* (co‑operation agreement) would be required, specifying the dates, routes to be prioritized for pedestrians, and any adjustments to bus/subway frequencies.

2. **Dirección de Seguridad Vial (Road‑Safety Directorate)** – oversees traffic‑control officers and signage. The event would need a *Ordenanza Municipal* (municipal ordinance) authorizing temporary pedestrian‑only zones and allocating traffic‑control personnel.

3. **Policía de la Ciudad (City Police)** – provides crowd‑control, emergency response, and enforcement of the temporary traffic regime. A *Carta de Compromiso* (letter of commitment) would formalize police deployment, shift schedules, and liaison with health services.

4. **Ministerio de Salud de la Ciudad (City Health Ministry)** – supplies ambulances, first‑aid stations, and health‑risk monitoring (e.g., heat‑stroke). A *Protocolo de Atención Médica* (medical‑response protocol) would be drafted, outlining the number of ambulances, placement of first‑aid points, and data‑sharing with the event app for incident reporting.

5. **Dirección de Espacio Público (Public‑Space Directorate)** – manages parks, plazas, and street furniture. Their involvement is needed for setting up information kiosks, prize booths, and temporary rest areas. A *Acuerdo de Uso de Espacio Público* would grant permission for these installations.

6. **Dirección de Tecnologías de la Información (IT Directorate)** – can facilitate the integration of the tracking app with the city’s open‑data portal, ensuring data privacy compliance (Ley de Protección de Datos Personales). A *Convenio de Intercambio de Datos* would define data‑ownership, anonymisation, and reporting requirements.

7. **Secretaría de Medio Ambiente (Environment Secretariat)** – can endorse the event, provide branding, and help quantify emissions reductions. A *Carta de Respaldo* would lend official legitimacy and may unlock environmental‑grant funding.

**Formal mechanisms** typically involve:
- **Municipal Ordinance** (Ordenanza) to grant temporary traffic‑management powers.
- **Inter‑agency Working Group** (Grupo de Trabajo Inter‑institucional) chaired by the Secretaría de Transporte, meeting monthly in the lead‑up to the event to coordinate logistics, communications, and risk management.
- **Memorandum of Understanding (MoU)** with private sponsors and NGOs, outlining responsibilities, branding rights, and data‑use clauses.
- **Budget Allocation** through the city’s annual plan, earmarked for security, health, and communications costs.

By anchoring the event within these existing structures and securing the listed agreements, the logistical risk is substantially reduced, which could raise the feasibility score toward **4** if the proposal commits to establishing the inter‑agency working group and formal MoUs well before the event date.

peer_answers.question: Could you specify which Argentine data‑protection regulations (e.g., Law 25.326) would apply to the location‑tracking app, and what concrete compliance measures (consent, anonymisation, data‑retention limits) the proposal would need to implement?
peer_answers.answer:
In Argentina the personal data regime is governed by Law No. 25.326 (Personal Data Protection Law) and its implementing regulations issued by the Dirección Nacional de Protección de Datos Personales. Location data are considered "sensitive personal data" because they can identify an individual's movements and habits. The proposal would therefore need to meet the following concrete obligations:

1. **Lawful basis and informed consent** – Before any GPS data are collected, the app must obtain explicit, informed consent from each participant. The consent form should be presented in clear, plain language and must describe the specific purposes (e.g., calculating distance walked, estimating GHG savings, publishing aggregated metrics). Participants must be able to withdraw consent at any time.

2. **Purpose limitation and data minimisation** – The app may only process location data that are strictly necessary for the stated purposes. Raw, high‑frequency traces should be aggregated (e.g., total distance per day) and personal identifiers (name, email) should be stored separately.

3. **Anonymisation / pseudonymisation** – To reduce re‑identification risk, the dataset released to sponsors or the media should be fully anonymised. This can be achieved by removing direct identifiers and applying statistical techniques (e.g., k‑anonymity) before any external sharing. Internally, a pseudonym (random ID) can link a participant’s data to their consent record.

4. **Security safeguards** – Data must be stored on encrypted servers (AES‑256 at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit). Access controls, audit logs, and regular vulnerability assessments are required to protect against unauthorised access.

5. **Data‑retention limits** – Law 25.326 mandates that personal data be kept only for the period necessary to fulfil the purpose. A reasonable retention schedule could be: raw location logs retained for the duration of the event plus a short analysis window (e.g., 30 days), aggregated results retained for up to 12 months for reporting, after which all personal data are deleted or fully anonymised.

6. **Rights of data subjects** – The app must provide mechanisms for participants to exercise their rights (access, rectification, deletion, data portability). A dedicated contact point (e‑mail or in‑app portal) should be offered.

7. **Data‑impact assessment (DPIA)** – Because the processing involves sensitive data, a DPIA should be carried out and, if required, submitted to the national authority before launch.

8. **Cross‑border transfers** – If any data are stored on servers outside Argentina, the transfer must comply with the law’s adequacy or contractual safeguards (e.g., Standard Contractual Clauses).

Implementing these measures would bring the app into compliance with Argentine law and mitigate legal risk. The proposal should explicitly budget for a data‑protection officer or legal counsel to design and audit these controls.

---

**Implication for the overall evaluation**: The current proposal does not address any of these requirements. Adding a concrete data‑protection plan would raise the feasibility score modestly (e.g., from 3 to 4) because it reduces a major regulatory risk, but only if the plan is realistically funded and staffed.

peer_answers.question: Given your concern about the lack of a detailed risk‑mitigation plan, what specific mitigation actions would you prioritize (e.g., contingency staffing, insurance, communication protocols) to reduce implementation risk for the event?
peer_answers.answer:
A robust risk‑mitigation framework should address three inter‑related domains: (1) operational safety, (2) stakeholder coordination, and (3) data/technology reliability. The following actions are the highest‑impact priorities for a one‑day city‑wide "Let’s Walk" event:

1. **Formal stakeholder agreements (MOUs)** – Secure written commitments from the municipal government, public‑transport operators, NGOs, and major employers. These MOUs should define roles, permit requirements, and escalation procedures. Clear agreements reduce the risk of last‑minute cancellations or conflicting traffic‑management decisions.

2. **Public‑liability and event insurance** – Obtain a comprehensive event‑insurance policy covering personal injury, property damage, and third‑party claims. The policy should also cover potential business interruption for participating companies.

3. **Contingency staffing and on‑site safety** – Deploy a minimum of 1 security officer and 1 medical responder per 5,000 participants (based on WHO crowd‑management guidelines). Provide first‑aid kits, AEDs, and a mobile command centre to coordinate emergency response.

4. **Redundant app infrastructure** – Host the tracking app on a cloud platform with automatic fail‑over across at least two geographic regions. Implement offline data capture (local caching) so that temporary loss of connectivity does not result in data loss. Schedule a pre‑event load‑test with a sample of 1,000 users to validate scalability.

5. **Data‑privacy safeguards (see answer to A2)** – Conduct a Data‑Impact Assessment, embed consent flows, and enforce encryption. A breach response plan (including notification timelines) should be documented.

6. **Communication and crisis‑management plan** – Develop a multi‑channel communication strategy (SMS alerts, social‑media updates, local radio) to inform participants of route changes, weather warnings, or emergencies. Designate a spokesperson and a rapid‑response team to handle media inquiries.

7. **Weather and environmental contingency** – Define trigger thresholds (e.g., temperature > 35 °C, severe rain) that would activate a "partial‑pause" protocol, including alternative indoor activities or a virtual participation option.

8. **Post‑event evaluation and feedback loop** – Allocate resources for a rapid de‑brief (within 48 h) to capture lessons learned, participant satisfaction, and any incident reports. This feedback is essential for scaling the initiative to other cities.

Prioritising these eight actions addresses the most salient risks identified in the proposal (lack of permits, safety of large crowds, data‑privacy exposure, and technology failure). Implementing them would substantially increase confidence in the event’s feasibility and could justify raising the feasibility score from 3 to 4, provided the budget and timeline accommodate these measures.

---

**Implication for the overall evaluation**: The proposal currently omits a risk‑mitigation plan, which is a key reason for the moderate feasibility rating. If the project team incorporates the above actions with realistic budgeting and staffing, the feasibility assessment should be revised upward. Until such details are supplied, the original scores remain unchanged.

peer_answers.question: Given the privacy concerns you highlighted, what concrete data‑governance safeguards (e.g., consent mechanisms, data minimisation, independent oversight) could be built into the tracking app to protect participants, especially those from vulnerable communities?
peer_answers.answer:
To protect participants – and especially those who are socially or economically vulnerable – the app must embed a layered governance framework that respects the principles of informed consent, data minimisation, purpose limitation, transparency, and independent oversight.

1. **Granular, informed consent**
   - Before any data collection begins, the app should present a short, plain‑language consent screen that explains *what* data will be collected (GPS coordinates, step count, mode of transport), *why* it is needed (to calculate avoided emissions), *how long* it will be stored, and *who* will have access (the project team, sponsors, and a designated data‑trust).
   - Consent must be opt‑in, not opt‑out, and users should be able to withdraw consent at any time, triggering immediate deletion of their raw location traces.
   - For participants with limited digital literacy, the consent process should be offered in multiple languages and formats (e.g., audio, visual icons) and reinforced through community outreach sessions.

2. **Data minimisation and aggregation**
   - Collect only the data points needed for the specific metric (e.g., total distance walked per day). Raw GPS traces should be discarded after they have been transformed into an aggregated distance figure.
   - Store data in an anonymised, pseudonymised form: replace personal identifiers with a random ID that cannot be linked back without a separate key held by an independent custodian.
   - Use differential privacy techniques when publishing aggregate statistics so that individual movement patterns cannot be re‑identified.

3. **Purpose limitation and retention policy**
   - The app’s privacy policy must state that data will be used *solely* for the "Let’s Walk" event reporting and for any follow‑up research that has been explicitly approved by the oversight board.
   - Raw data should be automatically deleted after a short, predefined retention window (e.g., 30 days) unless the participant consents to longer storage for research purposes.

4. **Independent oversight board**
   - Establish a multi‑stakeholder data‑governance board that includes representatives from:
     * Local NGOs that work with low‑income and disability communities
     * A municipal data‑privacy office
     * An academic ethics committee (e.g., ITBA’s Institutional Review Board)
     * A civil‑society digital‑rights organization
   - The board’s mandate is to review the app’s code, audit data handling practices, and approve any secondary uses of the data.

5. **Transparency and auditability**
   - Publish a concise, publicly accessible data‑handling summary that details the data flow diagram, retention periods, and security measures.
   - Conduct a third‑party security audit (penetration testing, code review) before launch and release the audit report in the public domain.

6. **Local data storage and sovereignty**
   - Host all servers within Argentine jurisdiction to ensure that data is subject to national privacy law (e.g., Ley de Protección de Datos Personales). This reduces the risk of cross‑border data exploitation by multinational sponsors.

7. **Community‑driven feedback loop**
   - Provide an in‑app channel for participants to report concerns, request data deletion, or suggest improvements. The oversight board must respond within a defined timeframe (e.g., 48 hours).

By embedding these safeguards, the project can mitigate the risk of disproportionate harm to vulnerable groups, build trust, and satisfy ethical standards without compromising the core goal of measuring emission reductions.

*Implication for the proposal’s feasibility*: Incorporating this governance framework adds a modest implementation cost and timeline (approximately 2–3 months for design, legal review, and audit). However, it substantially lowers the risk of legal challenges or public backlash, thereby raising the overall feasibility score from a tentative 3 to a more confident **4**.

peer_answers.question: Given the privacy issues you raised, what concrete data‑governance model (e.g., open‑source, independent audit, data‑trust) would you recommend to ensure the location data collected by the app remains transparent, secure, and under public oversight?
peer_answers.answer:
The most robust model for this type of civic‑engagement app is a **Hybrid Open‑Source Data‑Trust Architecture** that combines technical transparency with institutional accountability.

1. **Open‑source code base**
   - Release the full client‑side and server‑side source code under a permissive license (e.g., Apache 2.0). This allows any researcher, civil‑society group, or interested citizen to inspect how location data is captured, processed, and anonymised.
   - Host the repository on a public platform (GitHub or GitLab) with a clear contribution and issue‑tracking process.

2. **Data‑trust entity**
   - Create a legally recognised, non‑profit data‑trust (e.g., "Buenos Aires Climate Data Trust") that holds the raw data escrow. The trust’s charter should stipulate:
     * The data can only be accessed for the explicit purpose of the "Let’s Walk" event and approved follow‑up research.
     * Any secondary use requires a formal request reviewed by the independent oversight board (see answer 1).
   - The trust should be governed by a board that mirrors the oversight board composition: NGOs, municipal data‑privacy office, academic ethicists, and a digital‑rights advocate.

3. **Independent audit & certification**
   - Before launch, commission a reputable security firm to perform a full code audit, penetration test, and privacy‑impact assessment (PIA). The audit report must be published in full.
   - After the event, a post‑mortem audit should verify that raw location traces were deleted according to the retention policy and that only aggregated, anonymised metrics remain.

4. **Zero‑knowledge proof for emissions calculation**
   - To further reduce the need for raw data, the app can implement a cryptographic protocol where the device locally computes the distance walked and then generates a zero‑knowledge proof that the reported figure is within a plausible range. The server receives only the proof and the aggregated total, never the raw GPS trace.

5. **Data‑locality and sovereignty**
   - All data storage must reside on servers physically located in Argentina and operated by a trusted local cloud provider subject to national data‑protection regulations. This prevents foreign jurisdiction over the data.

6. **Public dashboard & open data**
   - Publish a real‑time, aggregated dashboard showing total kilometers walked, estimated emissions avoided, and participation rates. The data should be released in open formats (CSV/JSON) with a clear disclaimer that it is fully anonymised.

7. **Governance transparency**
   - The data‑trust’s annual report, financial statements, and board meeting minutes should be publicly accessible. This ensures that the trust does not become a back‑door for corporate sponsors to extract commercial value from the data.

**Why this model matters for equity**
- **Community control**: By placing the data under a trust co‑governed by local NGOs, the community retains agency over how their movement data is used.
- **Technical openness**: Open‑source code demystifies the algorithm that translates steps into emission savings, preventing opaque “black‑box” calculations that could misrepresent impact.
- **Legal safeguards**: Argentine data‑sovereignty rules and the trust’s charter protect vulnerable participants from cross‑border data exploitation.

Implementing this hybrid model would increase the proposal’s credibility and could raise the **impact** score modestly (from 2 to **3**) because transparent, trustworthy data collection makes the reported emission reductions more defensible and thus more likely to influence policy and future behaviour.

peer_answers.question: You note the need for a behavior‑change framework to sustain habits; could you suggest specific, low‑cost outreach or incentive strategies that would engage low‑income commuters and people in peripheral neighborhoods who may lack safe walking routes or bike access?
peer_answers.answer:
From a long‑term sustainability perspective, any lasting shift away from car‑dominant commuting must be rooted in equity‑focused, community‑driven interventions. Below are low‑cost strategies that can be layered onto the "Let’s Walk!" event to reach low‑income commuters and residents of peripheral districts:

1. **Neighborhood Walking Ambassadors**
   - Recruit local volunteers (e.g., community health workers, youth groups, senior centers) to act as "Walking Ambassadors" who organize short, safe walking loops on a weekly basis. Ambassadors receive modest stipends or in‑kind support (e.g., branded water bottles, reflective vests) funded by municipal grants or corporate CSR.
   - Ambassadors map existing sidewalks, parks, and low‑traffic streets using free tools (OpenStreetMap) and publish simple route cards in community centers and schools.

2. **Pop‑up Safe‑Route Improvements**
   - Partner with the city’s public works department to install temporary traffic‑calming measures (painted curb extensions, portable speed‑bump cones) on the most‑used walking corridors identified by the event’s app data. These interventions are inexpensive, reversible, and provide immediate safety signals.
   - Leverage local businesses to sponsor "Safe‑Street" signage in exchange for visibility on the event’s communication channels.

3. **Bike‑Share Access Vouchers**
   - Negotiate with the existing Ecobici system to allocate a limited pool of free‑ride vouchers for low‑income residents. Vouchers can be distributed through social‑service offices, schools, or community NGOs. The cost is minimal because vouchers are redeemed only when the bike is actually used.
   - Pair vouchers with a brief safety‑training session (15 minutes) delivered by volunteers, ensuring users feel confident on the bikes.

4. **Micro‑Incentive Challenges**
   - Use the event’s mobile app to run weekly "step‑or‑pedal" challenges that reward participants with small, tangible benefits: public‑transport passes, grocery store discounts, or community‑garden plot allocations. Rewards are sourced from local businesses that gain foot traffic and goodwill.
   - Design the challenges to be cumulative, encouraging participants to maintain the habit beyond the single‑day event.

5. **Community‑Led Storytelling**
   - Create short video or audio testimonials featuring residents from peripheral neighborhoods who have successfully integrated walking or biking into their daily routine. Distribute these stories via local radio, community Facebook groups, and WhatsApp chains—platforms that have high penetration in low‑income areas.
   - Storytelling builds social norms and reduces perceived barriers.

6. **Integration with Existing Social Programs**
   - Align the walking initiative with existing municipal programs such as "Healthy Neighborhoods" or "Urban Mobility for All." By embedding the event within broader policy frameworks, the city can allocate existing budget lines (e.g., for public‑health outreach) to support the activity without requiring new funding.

7. **Data‑Driven Route Prioritization**
   - After the event, analyze the app‑generated heat‑maps to identify neighborhoods with low participation but high potential (e.g., dense residential zones lacking safe sidewalks). Prioritize these areas for future low‑cost infrastructure upgrades (e.g., sidewalk repairs, street lighting) that have a lasting impact on walkability.

**Why these matter for long‑term resilience:**
- They address the equity gap by providing tangible resources (vouchers, safety improvements) where they are most needed.
- Community ownership through ambassadors and storytelling creates social reinforcement, a key driver of habit persistence.
- Low‑cost, reversible interventions allow the city to test and scale solutions without large capital outlays, reducing the risk of stranded investments.
- By linking walking incentives to existing social‑service networks, the program becomes part of a broader resilience strategy that simultaneously improves health, reduces emissions, and strengthens community cohesion.

Implementing a combination of these tactics would raise the feasibility of sustained behavior change, especially among the most vulnerable commuters, and would likely improve the overall impact score of the proposal.

peer_answers.question: Do you have suggestions for a post‑event monitoring framework that could detect rebound effects (e.g., increased car use) and ensure the claimed long‑term habit changes actually materialize?
peer_answers.answer:
A robust post‑event monitoring system must go beyond a one‑off data dump from the event app; it should track both individual behavior trajectories and system‑wide mobility patterns over time. The following framework blends low‑cost data sources with community verification to detect rebound effects and validate habit formation:

1. **Longitudinal Mobile‑App Cohort**
   - Keep the event app active for at least 12 months, inviting participants to opt‑in to a "Walking/Biking Tracker" module. Provide quarterly nudges (e.g., badge awards, small prizes) to maintain engagement.
   - Capture daily step counts, bike trips, and mode‑choice surveys ("Did you use a car for this trip?"), allowing the calculation of a personal carbon‑footprint trajectory.

2. **Passive Mobility Data Integration**
   - Partner with the city’s public‑transport authority to obtain anonymized smart‑card tap‑in/tap‑out data and vehicle count sensors on major arterials. Compare pre‑event baseline traffic volumes with post‑event trends on a monthly basis.
   - Use open‑source traffic‑count APIs (e.g., TomTom, Google Traffic) to monitor changes in average vehicle speed and congestion levels in key corridors.

3. **Household Travel Surveys**
   - Conduct a short, low‑cost telephone or door‑to‑door survey in a stratified sample of neighborhoods (including peripheral areas) at 3, 6, and 12 months after the event. Ask about primary commuting mode, frequency of walking/biking, and any recent changes in car use.
   - Cross‑validate self‑reported data with the app cohort to assess reporting bias.

4. **Rebound‑Effect Indicator Dashboard**
   - Define a set of leading indicators:
     * **Car‑Trip Ratio** = (Number of car trips per capita) / (Total trips per capita).
     * **Average Daily Kilometers by Car**.
     * **Walking/Biking Share** = (Walking + biking trips) / (Total trips).
   - Plot these indicators monthly. A statistically significant upward trend in the Car‑Trip Ratio after the event would flag a rebound.

5. **Community Observation Panels**
   - Establish neighborhood “Mobility Watch” groups (volunteers from local NGOs) that conduct brief weekly observations of traffic flow and pedestrian activity on key streets. Their qualitative notes complement quantitative data and can surface micro‑level rebound phenomena (e.g., increased weekend car trips for leisure).

6. **Feedback Loop to Policy Makers**
   - Produce a quarterly "Mobility Impact Report" summarizing the indicators, highlighting any rebound signals, and recommending corrective actions (e.g., temporary car‑free zones, additional bike‑share vouchers).
   - Present the report to the municipal transport department, health ministry, and sponsoring NGOs to keep the initiative on the policy agenda.

7. **Adaptive Incentive Mechanism**
   - If rebound is detected (e.g., a 5 % rise in car‑trip ratio over two consecutive months), trigger a responsive incentive: double the prize points for walking/biking that month, or introduce a limited‑time discount on public‑transport passes.
   - This adaptive loop creates a feedback‑controlled system that actively discourages back‑sliding.

**Why this framework supports long‑term sustainability:**
- **Multi‑source triangulation** reduces reliance on a single data stream, improving accuracy and credibility.
- **Temporal depth** (12‑month horizon) captures habit formation cycles and seasonal variations, essential for distinguishing temporary spikes from durable change.
- **Community involvement** ensures that monitoring is socially embedded, increasing trust and the likelihood of early detection of unintended consequences.
- **Policy‑ready outputs** (dashboards, reports) translate raw data into actionable insights, enabling city officials to adjust infrastructure or incentive programs in near‑real time.

By institutionalizing this monitoring architecture, the "Let’s Walk!" initiative can move from a one‑off awareness event to a continuous, evidence‑based program that demonstrably reduces emissions and mitigates rebound effects, thereby strengthening its long‑term impact and resilience.

peer_answers.question: What concrete metrics or methods would you use to evaluate whether the event disproportionately excludes low‑income residents who lack safe walking or biking infrastructure?
peer_answers.answer:
To spot exclusion we need disaggregated data that links participation to socio‑economic and spatial variables.  A practical set of metrics would be:
1. **Participation rate by income decile** – cross‑reference the app’s anonymised user IDs with the latest census blocks (or a city‑wide household income map) to calculate the % of participants from each income bracket.  A gap of more than 10 pp between the lowest and highest deciles would flag inequity.
2. **Geographic origin of participants** – map the start‑point of each tracked trip (or the nearest cell‑tower) and overlay it on a GIS layer of sidewalk and protected bike‑lane coverage.  Areas with < 30 % sidewalk coverage that also show low participation are likely being left out.
3. **Average trip distance vs. feasible walking/biking distance** – compute the median distance from home to work for participants and compare it to the distribution of distances for the whole working‑age population.  If low‑income residents have a median commute > 5 km while the event only rewards trips < 3 km, the design is biased.
4. **Safety perception survey** – conduct a short, on‑the‑ground questionnaire (or an online poll sent to residents in low‑income districts) asking about perceived safety of sidewalks, lighting, and traffic.  Scores below a defined threshold (e.g., 3 / 5) indicate a barrier.
5. **Incident reporting** – track any accidents or near‑misses reported to city safety services on the event day, broken down by neighbourhood.  A higher incident rate in poorer districts would signal unsafe infrastructure.
6. **Access to bikes** – count the number of eco‑bici stations within a 500 m radius of low‑income neighbourhoods and compare to usage logs.  Low station density coupled with low participation points to a structural gap.
By triangulating these quantitative layers (participation, distance, infrastructure, safety perception, incident data) we can produce an "Equity Exclusion Index" for the event and identify where targeted interventions (e.g., temporary pop‑up bike lanes, subsidised bike‑share vouchers, safety marshals) are needed.

If the index shows a significant disparity, the organizers should pause the rollout, allocate resources to improve infrastructure in the affected zones, and re‑measure before the next edition.

peer_answers.question: You highlighted equity and safety concerns; could you elaborate on mechanisms the proposal could adopt to monitor and mitigate possible rebound effects, where participants might increase car travel on non‑event days after the walk day?
peer_answers.answer:
Rebound is a real risk: a one‑off "walk day" can create a sense of having "done my part" and lead to more car trips later.  To keep the behavioural shift alive, the project should embed a few low‑cost monitoring and feedback loops:
1. **Longitudinal travel tracking** – keep the same anonymised app active for a month after the event and record weekly car‑kilometre equivalents (using the same footprint algorithm).  Compare the post‑event average to a baseline month before the event.  A rise of > 5 % would trigger a mitigation response.
2. **Post‑event surveys** – ask participants whether they plan to keep walking/biking more often and what barriers they face.  Use the responses to adjust employer policies (e.g., more flexible home‑office days) and city services (e.g., improved bike lanes).
3. **Tiered incentives** – instead of a single prize for the event day, introduce a "sustained impact" badge that rewards users who log a minimum number of low‑carbon trips per week for the next 4‑6 weeks.  This keeps the gamified motivation alive beyond the day itself.
4. **Employer commitments** – require participating companies to sign a short‑term pledge (e.g., one additional home‑office day per month) and report compliance.  The app can verify reduced commuting trips for employees of those firms.
5. **Public‑transport integration** – coordinate with the transit authority to offer discounted tickets or free rides for participants who log a certain number of bike/walk trips in the weeks after the event.  This creates a positive substitution rather than a rebound to cars.
6. **Community‑level feedback dashboards** – publish a simple weekly chart showing city‑wide car‑km saved versus any uptick, so the public can see the collective impact and feel accountable.  Transparency discourages "slack" behaviour.
7. **Targeted outreach in high‑car‑use zones** – identify neighbourhoods where car use spikes after the event (using the same GIS analysis as in answer 1) and run micro‑campaigns (e.g., pop‑up bike‑share stations, safe‑route walks) to keep the momentum.
By combining continuous data collection, adaptive incentives, and employer‑city partnerships, the proposal can detect a rebound early and intervene before it erodes the climate and health gains achieved on the walk day.

These mechanisms also provide the evidence base needed to refine the event design for future editions, making the initiative more inclusive and resilient.

feasibility.rationale: The Q&A clarified that a city‑scale tracking app can be built for roughly USD 250‑375k and funded through municipal innovation funds, PPPs, or climate grants. Existing Buenos Aires inter‑agency frameworks (Transport Secretariat, Road Safety Directorate, City Police, Health Ministry, etc.) can provide the required permits and operational support if formal MOUs and a municipal ordinance are secured. The proposal still hinges on achieving a 5 % participation rate, which empirical evidence suggests is optimistic, but the added risk‑mitigation plan (insurance, contingency staffing, redundant app infrastructure, data‑privacy safeguards) substantially lowers implementation risk, justifying an upgraded feasibility rating.

novelty.rationale: One‑day car‑free or walk‑to‑work events and gamified tracking apps are already common worldwide; the proposal adds only a modest combination of incentives and a local data‑driven awareness angle, which does not constitute a fundamentally new approach.

impact.rationale: Using a more realistic Argentine car emission factor (0.13‑0.18 kg CO₂e km⁻¹) would increase the per‑km savings, but the assumed 5 % city‑wide participation is not supported by prior Latin‑American pilots (typically 0.3‑2.7 %). Consequently, the projected 66 tCO₂e/day and ~106 ktCO₂e/yr are likely overestimates. Even with lower uptake, the event could generate measurable short‑term emissions reductions and longer‑term habit change, placing the overall impact at a moderate level with high uncertainty.

presentation.rationale: The document remains poorly organized, contains typographical errors, and mixes timelines and stakeholder lists, making verification difficult despite the additional clarifications provided in the Q&A.

A1.perspective_summary: From a scientific‑systems viewpoint, LET'S WALK! is a plausible pilot that leverages existing mobility infrastructure and can be supported by a realistic budget and inter‑agency coordination. However, the novelty is limited, the emissions‑reduction claims rely on optimistic participation assumptions, and the proposal’s presentation hampers rapid assessment. Addressing data‑privacy compliance and a detailed risk‑mitigation plan improves feasibility, but impact remains uncertain.
key_concerns: Data‑privacy compliance with Argentina's Law 25.326 (consent, anonymisation, retention limits) is currently absent.
Achieving the 5 % participation target is not supported by empirical evidence from similar events in Buenos Aires and other Latin‑American cities.
